What the Hell Is .DS_Store? Should You Commit It to Git?
.DS_Store is a hidden file macOS auto-creates in every folder, storing display settings like icon positions and window layout. Finder won't show it — only
; most framework gitignore templates already ignore it by default.
What is .DS_Store? Should you commit it to Git? Why does it even exist? Let's go through it.
.DS_Store is a hidden file that macOS auto-creates. The name stands for "
". If you're a Windows dev, think of it as the macOS version of
— though this post is only about .DS_Store, so Windows folks can sit this one out.
It's hidden, which means even when you toggle hidden files in Finder with
That's because Finder deliberately doesn't display .DS_Store. To see it, you have to open Terminal and run:
command to check what kind of file it is:

So what does .DS_Store actually contain? Basically: folder icon positions, window settings, Finder view preferences, that kind of thing. Because of that, pretty much every folder on macOS has one. They don't take up much space though, so most of the time you can just ignore them.
The answer here is pretty obvious —
. Like I just mentioned, .DS_Store records local info, so committing it can (in theory) leak some stuff you didn't intend to. The risk is low, but it's there.
That's why most CLI scaffolding tools include .DS_Store in their default .gitignore. Here's Vite's, for example:

# Logs
logs
*.log
npm-debug.log*
yarn-debug.log*
yarn-error.log*
pnpm-debug.log*
lerna-debug.log*

node_modules
dist
dist-ssr
*.local

# Editor directories and files
.vscode/*
!.vscode/extensions.json
.idea
.DS_Store
*.suo
*.ntvs*
*.njsproj
*.sln
*.sw?
To keep things clean, you should always add .DS_Store to your .gitignore.
You can also tell macOS to stop creating .DS_Store files on network volumes. Open Terminal and run:
Restart again after running this one.
And if you want to find and delete every existing .DS_Store on your Mac:
Honestly, I wouldn't recommend deleting or disabling it. You
do it without breaking anything, sure.

But unless you have a specific reason, .DS_Store doesn't hurt performance, doesn't usually pose a real security risk, and it's actually doing something useful — recording your folder display preferences. Delete it and you'll lose some of those settings.
So that one's up to you.
One thing that's clear though: to protect yourself, .DS_Store should always be in your .gitignore.

What actually is .DS_Store? Should it go in your Git repo? Why does it even exist? This post walks through what .DS_Store is, why every macOS folder has one, and why you should keep it out of version control.
